worth_it_explanation: Yahoo DSP access is seat-based: a seat license grants a user the ability to view, create, and update objects across every advertiser account that belongs to that seat. When roles are assigned broadly, or when accounts for departed employees and former agency contacts are left active, the resulting access model is wider than the work it needs to support. These checks are fast to run against the seat's user list, the fix is a role adjustment or a user removal, and the outcome is an access model that reflects who actually manages the account today.
intro: Yahoo DSP manages access through a seat-based model. Each user is provisioned on one or more seat licenses, and the seat license determines which advertiser accounts that user can reach. Within a seat, specific roles control what a user can do: a user with the User Ops role manages other users on the seat, a user with the AdvertiserManager role can edit campaigns and settings for a specific advertiser, and a user with the Advertiser Report role can pull reports for that advertiser without making edits. These checks confirm that the roles assigned match each person's actual job function, that accounts for departed employees and former partners have been removed, and that users who only need to review reporting are not holding roles that also let them edit live campaigns and budgets.

audit_checks.title: Users hold broader roles than their work requires
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When edit-capable roles are assigned as a default rather than limited to users who actually create or modify campaigns, anyone on the seat can alter live line items, budgets, or targeting. An accidental change from a user whose work is limited to reviewing pacing or pulling reports is harder to detect and reverse than one made by a user whose scope matches their responsibilities.

educate.base:
Yahoo DSP (demand-side platform) controls what each user can do through a role assigned at the seat or advertiser level. The AdvertiserManager role lets a user edit campaigns, line items, and settings for the advertiser accounts it covers. The Advertiser Report role provides reporting access to those same accounts without granting the ability to make edits. Users with the User Ops role can add, edit, or remove other users on the seat, which is a distinct administrative capability separate from campaign management.

Assigning AdvertiserManager broadly, rather than to the users who actually build and manage campaigns, creates a wider surface for unintentional changes. A media analyst who needs to review performance data does not need the ability to edit live campaigns or adjust budgets. A client contact who monitors reporting does not need a role that also lets them modify targeting or creative assignments. When roles are set to the minimum level each person's work requires, the impact of a mistake stays contained to the user who made it.

A few patterns point to roles being broader than needed:
  • Users who pull reports or monitor pacing hold the AdvertiserManager role when Advertiser Report would cover their work
  • The User Ops role is assigned to users who do not perform user management tasks on the seat
  • Multiple users hold edit-capable roles on advertisers they are not actively managing
You may want to review each user's role against their actual job function before making adjustments. The role mismatch, not the role name itself, is the signal.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, a campaign manager running retargeting or prospecting campaigns needs the AdvertiserManager role for those advertiser accounts. A merchandising or analytics contact who reviews conversion performance typically needs Advertiser Report only. You may want to confirm that reporting-only contacts do not hold AdvertiserManager access that lets them edit campaign settings or budgets.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, the person building and optimizing line items for form-submission campaigns needs AdvertiserManager access. A sales or CRM contact reviewing lead volume in reports needs Advertiser Report only. You may want to confirm that contacts whose task is reading data are not holding a role that also allows edits.

educate.learn_more_note: Detailed documentation on Yahoo DSP user roles and permission levels is available in the Yahoo DSP Help Center under 'Manage Users.' Access to that section requires a Yahoo DSP seat login. Your Yahoo account team can provide guidance on the current role model if you do not have direct access to the Help Center.

investigate.intro: Review the full user list on the seat and compare each user's assigned role against their actual job function.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Sign in to Yahoo DSP with User Ops access. In the seat navigation, open 'Seat Settings' and select the 'Users' section.
Review the full list of users, their assigned roles, and the advertiser accounts each role covers. Note any user holding the AdvertiserManager role.

For each AdvertiserManager user, confirm whether they actively create or modify campaigns, line items, or targeting settings. A user whose work is limited to reviewing pacing or pulling reports is a candidate for the Advertiser Report role.

For each User Ops user, confirm whether they perform user management tasks on the seat. If a user holds User Ops but does not add, edit, or remove other users, that role may be broader than their work requires.

Note any users with edit-capable roles on advertiser accounts they are not actively managing. A user with AdvertiserManager access to an advertiser they have no active campaigns on is a candidate for role removal or downgrade on that advertiser.

evaluate.logic: For each user flagged during the review, record their name, current role, the advertiser accounts covered, and whether the role matches their actual responsibilities. A user whose role matches their job function passes. A user whose role grants more access than their work requires is a fail, recorded with the user's name, current role, and the recommended adjustment.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user's name, current role, the advertiser accounts covered, and whether the role matches their responsibilities to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that several users in {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP seat hold roles broader than their actual responsibilities require. Users whose work is limited to reviewing campaign performance or pulling reports hold the AdvertiserManager role, which also lets them edit live campaigns, line items, and budgets they are not responsible for. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the full user list against each person's job function and adjusting users whose work is limited to reporting to the Advertiser Report role. For users who hold the User Ops role without performing user management tasks, {{voice_recommend}} confirming whether that role is still needed before the next access review.

audit_checks.title: Departed or inactive users still hold seat access
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Yahoo DSP does not expire or remove user access automatically when an employee leaves, a contract ends, or an agency relationship changes. A seat account that remains active after the person has moved on retains full access to campaign data, audience configurations, and advertiser settings at whatever role it was last assigned.

educate.base:
Yahoo DSP seat access does not expire automatically. When a user's employment ends, a contract concludes, or an agency relationship changes, their seat account stays active at the same role and advertiser scope it held at the time, until a user with User Ops access explicitly removes it. The credentials remain usable for as long as the account exists in the seat's user list.

A former team member who held the AdvertiserManager role retains the ability to edit campaigns, line items, targeting, and creative assignments for the advertiser accounts in their scope. A former User Ops holder retains the ability to manage other users on the seat. The account remains fully functional regardless of whether the person is still part of the organization or agency.

Stale access reaches a seat through several paths. An offboarding process that disables the corporate email or revokes VPN access does not propagate to the Yahoo DSP seat. An agency roster change that removes a contact from the agency's own systems does not automatically remove them from the client's seat. A vendor contact who rotated off the account months ago remains listed in the seat's user section with the same scope they held at the start of the engagement.

A few patterns point to stale access in the seat user list:
  • User accounts associated with email addresses at agencies or vendors the advertiser no longer works with
  • User accounts for email addresses that no longer exist in the client's identity provider or HR system
  • User accounts that have not been updated in an extended period and whose current role assignment cannot be matched to an active team member
You may want to cross-reference the seat user list against the client's current employee roster, active agency contacts, and current vendor relationships before removing any account. A user who appears inactive may still hold a legitimate role.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, former agency contacts who ran past display retargeting or prospecting campaigns may still hold AdvertiserManager access to the advertiser, including visibility into product-level campaign structures and conversion event configurations. You may want to confirm that access for past agencies was explicitly removed when the relationship ended.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a former agency or vendor contact with AdvertiserManager access can view lead volume data, audience configurations, and campaign targeting built around form-submission audiences. You may want to confirm that contacts no longer part of the account team have been removed from the seat.

educate.learn_more_note: Documentation on removing users and managing seat access in Yahoo DSP is available in the Yahoo DSP Help Center under 'Manage Users.' Access to that section requires a Yahoo DSP seat login. Your Yahoo account team can walk through the user removal process if you do not have direct access to the Help Center.

investigate.intro: Pull the full user list on the seat and cross-reference it against the client's current roster of active employees, agency contacts, and vendor relationships.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: In the seat's 'Seat Settings', open 'Users' and review the complete user list: name, email address, role, and the advertiser accounts covered.
Review each email domain against the client's current agency roster and vendor list. Flag any domain that belongs to an agency or vendor the client no longer works with.
For each flagged user, confirm with the client's account owner whether the person still has a legitimate reason to hold seat access. Remove users the client confirms are no longer active.
Cross-reference the remaining user list against the client's current employee directory. Flag any accounts for email addresses that no longer exist in the client's identity provider.

Note the last-updated date for each user record if the DSP UI surfaces it. Accounts that have not been modified in an extended period and cannot be matched to an active team member are candidates for confirmation and removal.

evaluate.logic: For each flagged user, record their name, email domain, current role, the advertiser accounts covered, and whether the client confirms they are still active. A user confirmed as currently active passes. A user the client cannot confirm, or who is associated with a former agency or vendor relationship, is a fail recorded with the user's name, email, role, and scope.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each flagged user's name, email, role, advertiser scope, and active-or-departed status to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} user accounts in {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP seat that belong to contacts who are no longer part of the active account team, or whose current association with the account cannot be confirmed. These accounts retain the roles and advertiser-level access they were assigned when the relationship was active. {{voice_recommend}} cross-referencing the full seat user list against the client's current employee directory and agency roster, removing any account the client confirms is no longer active, and adding an explicit Yahoo DSP seat user removal step to the offboarding process whenever a team member, agency contact, or vendor rotates off the account.

audit_checks.title: No separation between users who can edit campaigns and those who only report
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When all seat users hold edit-capable roles and no reporting-only role is in use, a budget change, targeting adjustment, or line item pause made by someone whose task was only to review performance has no structural barrier to prevent it. For accounts with active campaign spend, the absence of role separation means any user reviewing the account can also alter it.

educate.base:
Yahoo DSP provides distinct roles for users who manage campaigns and users who only review reporting. The AdvertiserManager role covers campaign creation, editing, and settings management for the advertiser accounts in its scope. The Advertiser Report role covers reporting access only, without the ability to edit campaigns, adjust bids or budgets, or change targeting.

When the Advertiser Report role is not in use and all seat users hold the AdvertiserManager role, there is no structural separation between the users who are supposed to make changes and the users who are only supposed to review performance. A client-side contact reviewing campaign pacing, an analyst monitoring frequency and viewability, or a finance contact confirming budget pacing each holds the same access level as the campaign manager making live adjustments.

The risk is not theoretical: an unintentional campaign pause, a budget cap adjustment, or a targeting change made by a user who thought they were only reading data can affect in-flight spend. Yahoo DSP logs changes by user, so the error is traceable, but the change itself may have already altered performance before it is caught.

A few patterns suggest reporting-only roles are not being used:
  • Every user on the seat holds the AdvertiserManager role regardless of job function
  • Client-side contacts, analytics reviewers, or finance approvers are provisioned at the same role level as campaign managers
  • The seat user list has no entries with the Advertiser Report role
You may want to identify which users' work is limited to reviewing data and move them to the Advertiser Report role. That adjustment does not reduce what they can see; it only removes the ability to make changes.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, it is worth checking whether analytics or merchandising contacts who review campaign performance against product-level goals hold edit-capable roles alongside the campaign managers who build and manage the campaigns. Moving reporting-only contacts to Advertiser Report keeps their view access intact while removing the ability to edit campaigns or budgets.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a sales team contact or CRM manager who monitors lead volume and cost-per-lead in Yahoo DSP reports typically does not need to edit campaigns. You may want to confirm that contacts whose task is reading performance data hold the Advertiser Report role rather than AdvertiserManager.

educate.learn_more_note: Documentation on the Advertiser Report role and how reporting-only access is configured in Yahoo DSP is available in the Yahoo DSP Help Center under 'Manage Users.' Access to that section requires a Yahoo DSP seat login. Your Yahoo account team can confirm the current role options and their permissions if you do not have direct access to the Help Center.

investigate.intro: Review the seat user list to confirm whether the Advertiser Report role is in use and whether it is assigned to users whose work is limited to reviewing reporting.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: In 'Seat Settings', open 'Users' and review the role column for each user. Note whether any users hold the Advertiser Report role, or whether all users hold the AdvertiserManager role.

For each AdvertiserManager user, identify their job function: campaign manager, analyst, client contact, finance reviewer, or other. Users whose function is review or reporting only are candidates for the Advertiser Report role.

Confirm with the account lead which users are responsible for making live campaign changes, such as edits to budgets, bids, targeting, or creative assignments. Users outside that list who hold AdvertiserManager access may be over-provisioned.

Note any client-side or agency-side contacts whose primary interaction with the account is reviewing reports or approving budgets. These contacts rarely need edit access and are the first candidates for an Advertiser Report role assignment.

evaluate.logic: Determine whether the Advertiser Report role is assigned to any seat user. If no user holds a reporting-only role and all users hold edit-capable roles regardless of job function, that is a fail. Record for each user whose function is reporting or review: their name, current role, and whether a reporting-only role would cover their work. A seat where at least one user is correctly scoped to a reporting-only role and the AdvertiserManager role is limited to users who actively manage campaigns passes.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user's name, role, and job function to confirm whether edit-capable and reporting-only roles are appropriately separated.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP seat does not separate users who manage campaigns from users who only review reporting. All users on the seat hold edit-capable roles regardless of their job function, meaning client contacts, analysts, and finance reviewers can modify live campaign budgets, targeting, and line item settings alongside the campaign managers responsible for those changes. {{voice_recommend}} identifying users whose work is limited to reviewing performance data and reassigning them to the Advertiser Report role, which preserves their full reporting access while removing the ability to make live edits. {{voice_recommend}} reserving AdvertiserManager access for users who actively create or modify campaigns.
